ground_truth.title: Was World War II really that bad?
ground_truth.main_content:
Greg Kemnitz
Former Staff Database Engineer at Big Tech (2020–2024)

I’ve been in full WWII history nerd mode the past few weeks while watching the “Second World War Diary” on Amazon Prime. It goes through every single day of the war from 1939 to 1945, spending two minutes per calendar day or so (so each month is an hour-long episode).

It has something like 72 hours of narrated video in a sort of summary newsreel format**.

One thing this format drills into you is there was … so much fighting and dying every day. “Today 4000 Jews were shot and killed someplace in Poland. Another few thousand were shipped to Auschwitz/Treblinka/wherever. Today five merchant ships were sunk. Today bomber raids dropped X thousands of pounds of bombs on London/Berlin/Munich/Coventry/Paris/Rome/Rabaul/Port Moresby/Tokyo/Yokohama/… and killed X thousands of people.”

This daily litany ends up giving you a pounding sense of the “routine” scale and general awfulness of the war.

It also gives you a sense of the global scope of the war that other documentaries don’t have time to cover:

There were battles in Madagascar (over the French fleet stationed there), as well as a ton of complexity regarding “Vichy-loyal” governors of French colonies (often with bits of the French fleet stationed in ports) versus others who supported De Gaulle’s Free French.

There were two distinct wars between the Soviet Union and Finland. Yes, Finns obviously know this, but a lot of more casual WWII history nerds know little about these.

The Italian campaign was a huge, bloody mess.

As was Burma.

As was Southeast Asia.

There were slow-burn wars in places like New Guinea and other islands in the area, even before the US started its “island-hopping” campaign.

Rare among English-language documentaries, it does a decent job covering fighting in China.

So, yes, WWII was “really that bad”.

**Unfortunately, there are a ton of weird oddities like inconsistent spellings (how many ways can you misspell Chungking or Chongqing, the wartime capital of China? Pick a spelling and stick with it.) and the refusal of narrators to use the English hard G, so he insists on calling the Geheime Staatspolizei aka Gestapo “the Jestapo”, and he also pronounces the Gilbert Islands as “Jilbert islands”, but otherwise it’s an awesome series and a must for any WWII history nerd…

Mats Andersson
Common or Garden Armchair Historian

Thanks for the A2A.

No, it was far worse.

Look, all the books you’ve read, all the documentaries you’ve been watching, and all the history books in the world, present an insanely sanitized version for you. They don’t show you the really gruesome photos. They don’t show you the sheer amount of bloodshed. They don’t show you the aftermath, when all of Europe starved for two to five years, but everyone was so grateful that they were no longer shot at that no one complained.

It was insanely brutal. It traumatized two generations. It targeted civilians on a totally unprecedented scale.

It had fucking industrial mass murder as a fucking war objective.

You can sit there in a comfortable home and type away, and say “not all that bad”, because you don’t slip on the intestines of a young woman walking to your desk, you don’t have babies skewered on bayonets outside the window, and you don’t have three good recipes for cat because it’s the only meat you’ve had this year, and when you go outdoors, you don’t have to pass the corpses of three classmates frozen solid and plunged head first into a snow drift to intimidate you.

You’re not edgy. You’re not funny. You’re not even a pathetic attempt at being a troll.

Ben Michael
Geography, history, and humanities enthusiast

Yes. Around 75 million people are thought to have died due to the Second World War, this was 3% of the entire world’s population. World War II didn’t just effect the men who went off to fight in Europe, it affected men, women, children, infants, soldiers, leaders, generals, beggars, and businessmen. On all sides people died, atrocities so awful we’re committed that we are inclined to call the people who committed them monsters.

I come from the United Kingdom, and have lived in England my whole life. I don’t have any family in or who were in the military, my great grandfather fought in Myanmar (then Burma) against the Japanese in the Second World War, and that is my only personal connection to that conflict. Now the UK was not affected as badly as many countries during the Second World War, being that their was sea between it and the axis powers, but that does not stop WWII holding a massive place in our cultural memory. You can’t go two weeks without hearing about ‘The War’ (and we know which war that refers to). In school we’d go on trips to see things associated with the war, we’d be given lessons about it, we’d look at black and white photos of the blitz and try on gas marks and the like. The Second World War rears its massive, ugly head over the British collective consciousness - I cannot imagine what it is like to someone from country like Poland, that lost far more that the United Kingdom ever did.

Johan Stander
Former Combat Veteran at South African National Defence Force (1981–1983)

Propably worse than anything a sane person can imagine. Think about the 2 dictatorships that was protected by their secret police with policy in conquered countries enforced by them.

The NKVD and Gestapo was not only ruthless but cold-blooded and did not care what their orders was but just to carry them out.

Point is that you were probably better of in most countries being a soldier than a civilian and surrendering was sometimes worse than dying.

The artillery was maybe less than in the 1st World War but more effective and this was made worse by Air power that was available on an unimaginable scale and whilst most forces did not target civilians they became collateral damage. And now add famine into the picture with resources a weapon of war.

And if you were just perceived to be of an inferior race or not dedicated to the cause you had problems.

Staying in the US or the colonies was much better, in the east however you were also prone to suffer from starvation as resources were taken to provide for the War effort and the individual, well he basically had few rights and the war effort took precedence over all.

Berliners used to say “enjoy the war the peace will be worse” and they were right up to a point as the Soviets had a free reign after the war.

Erik-Jan Buikema
Arduous World War II reader

Yes, it was really that bad.

WW II caused 70–80 million deaths in less than 6 years. That’s an average of 34,000 deaths per day. Go try and find a similar horror show.

With over 70 countries involved, global statistics are difficult to find. Below are the most relevant ones:

Almost all casualties refer to countries of the Asian (30–35 million) or European (40–45 million) hemisphere. Deaths from other nationalities were much less significant (0.5 million).

In total, 127 million soldiers were mobilized during the war, of which more than 15% perished.

Even more astonishing was the number of killed civilians: between 45 and 55 millions. Around half of these were deliberate murders, an incredible statistic fueled by the morally bankrupt German and Japanese regimes. For its part, Allied civilian bombing may have caused nearly a million casualties.

Famine and hunger-related diseases were the cause of 20–25 million deaths, including soldiers in captivity. In the Soviet Union 8 million people died from hunger, and a not well-known case is Indonesia, where more than 3 million people perished from starvation. Famine continued after the war ended, leading to an additional million of post-war casualties.

An important reason behind the lack of food was the disruption of sea trade. More than 20,000 ships got lost worldwide, with 15,000 of them being sunk. One third of all losses was caused by submarines.

Finally, 65 million people lost their houses in Europe. No figures are available for Asia, but in China alone there were 35 million displaced persons, and in Japan 8.5 million. The global number of people without a home could easily have been in the region of 120 million, and led to the highest scale of evacuations and refugee camps ever seen.

Andy Duffell
Ex-air force armourer

It was easily the worst event in history. The sheer scale of human suffering and death was greater than anything before or since, by a significant margin. Around 80 million dead in around 6 years (add on quite a few million more if you think the war really started in China in the mid-30s), plus hundreds of millions wounded, homeless and psychological casualties. Empires were lost, new atrocities and terrors were unleashed on mankind (extermination camps, atomic weapons, the firebombing of cities, etc). It was humanity at our absolute worst, and sometimes at our absolute best too.

The world today is still largely shaped by the status quo at the end of the war.

Ronald Mills
20 years active duty US Air Force Aircraft Structures Tech

The deadliest and most destructive conflict in our species entire history?
Yeah, it really was “that bad”.
